explanation: The requirement 'The user shall be able to disable/enable the leak compensation feature at any time.' states that at any moment, the user can choose to either enable or disable the leak compensation feature. There are two relevant atomic propositions: 'enableLeakCompensation', which represents the user enabling the feature, and negation thereof ('!enableLeakCompensation'), representing disabling it. The key part is allowing both actions 'at any time.' In LTL, this means that at all moments, it should always be possible to (eventually) enable or disable the feature. This is most closely captured by saying: globally, it is always possible to eventually enable leak compensation (GF enableLeakCompensation), and globally it is always possible to eventually disable it (GF !enableLeakCompensation). This captures the liveness property that neither state is permanently unreachable: the user can always eventually change the status as desired, at any time. Other LTL properties, such as just 'GF enableLeakCompensation' (without the disabling) or insisting on instantaneous toggling (which is not required here), would not be faithful to the requirement; the important part is the property of switchability at any time.

The input statement requires that the user must be able to enable or disable the leak compensation feature at any time. In terms of atomic propositions, the ability to enable and disable corresponds to the proposition 'enableLeakCompensation' being set (enabled) or not set (disabled) by the user at any time. Since there is no direct proposition for 'disableLeakCompensation', the enabling and disabling will be interpreted as: it should always be possible for the user to (eventually) enable leak compensation and it should always be possible for the user to (eventually) not have leak compensation enabled (i.e., it can be disabled). In LTL, this is best captured as 'Globally, eventually enableLeakCompensation' AND 'Globally, eventually NOT enableLeakCompensation', meaning from every moment, it is possible to reach a point where leak compensation is enabled, and possible to reach a point where it is not enabled (disabled). This expresses the toggling ability at all times, i.e., 'the user shall be able to enable or disable at any time.'

The requirement states that the user should be able to disable or enable the leak compensation feature at any time. In terms of atomic propositions, 'enableLeakCompensation' represents enabling leak compensation. The property must allow either disabling or enabling at any point in time. In LTL, 'eventually' (F) enables something at any time, but to capture that this possibility is always available (at any time, the user can choose to enable it again, and at any time, the user can choose to disable it again), it's best captured as: 'Globally, it is always possible to enable or disable leak compensation.' Since we only have an atomic proposition representing 'enable' (but not 'disable'), we'll interpret enabling/disabling as being able to trigger 'enableLeakCompensation' at any moment, i.e., at any point, eventually in the future, the user can choose to enable leak compensation. This is expressed as: G(F(enableLeakCompensation)). This means that no matter when, you can always in the future re-enable leak compensation (i.e., the ability to enable is always at hand). This captures the core of the requirement in terms of the given atomic propositions.

The natural language requirement states that the user shall be able to enable or disable the leak compensation feature at any time. Given the available atomic propositions, there is 'enableLeakCompensation' to indicate user enables leak compensation, but no explicit 'disableLeakCompensation'. To capture the user's freedom to enable or disable the feature at any time, the best LTL property is: globally, it should always be possible in the future for the user to enable leak compensation, and likewise, always possible in the future to not have it enabled. Since we do not have a direct proposition for user disables leak compensation, we use 'leakCompensationActive' to indicate the feature is on, so its negation means it's off. Thus, at any point, the user can eventually get to a state where leak compensation is active, and can eventually get to a state where it is not active. Therefore, the property is: G(F leakCompensationActive) & G(F !leakCompensationActive). Previous candidate properties may have focused solely on the ability to enable, but not explicitly also to disable (i.e., return to a state where it is not active), or may have failed to express 'at any time'. This property ensures both are always possible at any time by using the nested globally-eventually pattern.

The input natural language specifies that the user can enable or disable the leak compensation feature at any time, which means the user has the freedom to perform either action, whenever desired. The closest atomic proposition for enabling this feature is 'enableLeakCompensation'. However, there is not a corresponding 'disableLeakCompensation' proposition explicitly given, nor an explicit user action atomic proposition (e.g., 'userEnablesLeakComp'), and no proposition referencing the capability for toggling. Since we cannot use numerical comparisons or invent new atomic propositions, the best interpretation using the available APs is to state that it is always possible (at any time) for the user to enable leak compensation. In LTL, 'at any time the user shall be able to enable' is best expressed as 'G F enableLeakCompensation' (globally, eventually, enableLeakCompensation), which means from any point in time, it's always possible, somewhere in the future, to enable the leak compensation. This captures the spirit of the requirement that it is always possible to enable or (by symmetry, disable) the feature. If a 'disableLeakCompensation' AP were present, the formula would include it as well. Previous possible translations that simply stated 'G (enableLeakCompensation)' would require it to always be on, which does not match the input. Thus, 'G F enableLeakCompensation' most closely matches the 'at any time' capability to enable the feature based on available APs.

The requirement states that the leak compensation feature shall be able to be enabled or disabled by the user at any time. This means that there must always be the possibility for the user to issue an enable or disable action, i.e., enabling or disabling should always be available, unconstrained by system state. The atomic proposition 'enableLeakCompensation' represents that the user enables leak compensation. Since there is no explicit 'disableLeakCompensation' proposition, we interpret toggling as using 'enableLeakCompensation' repeatedly—as, in typical toggle mechanisms, the same action does enable or disable alternately. Thus, the property should express that it is always possible, from any point in time, for 'enableLeakCompensation' to occur again (i.e., the user is never locked out from making this choice in the future). This is captured by the property: 'G F enableLeakCompensation', meaning 'at all times, it will eventually be possible for the user to (re-)enable leak compensation', which, for a toggling feature, means the user is never prevented from interacting with the feature at any time.

The natural language requirement states that the user must be able to disable or enable the leak compensation feature at any time. This implies that the action of enabling or disabling the feature (user enables leak compensation, represented by enableLeakCompensation) is always possible to perform. Since the atomic propositions do not include a distinct 'disable' action (only 'enableLeakCompensation'), the LTL can only reference the enabling action. To capture at any time, the temporal operator 'eventually' (F) is appropriate since it means that, from any point in time, it is always possible to eventually enable leak compensation. To express this globally, we use the 'globally' operator (G) applied to 'eventually enableLeakCompensation': G(F(enableLeakCompensation)). This captures the intent that whenever the user wants, leak compensation can be enabled, though it cannot capture the 'disable' aspect explicitly unless a 'disableLeakCompensation' atomic proposition existed. Thus, this LTL property best approximates the provided natural language given the available atomic propositions.

The input states that the user must be able to disable or enable the leak compensation feature at any time. This means there should be no temporal restriction on when 'enableLeakCompensation' can become true (enabled) or false (disabled); it must be possible at any point in the system's operation. In LTL, the way to capture 'can be done at any time' is to require that it is always eventually possible to realize both states (enabled or disabled). In other words, it should always be possible to eventually enable it, and always be possible to eventually disable it, no matter what has happened previously. We model 'enableLeakCompensation' indicating that the user has enabled it (and by !enableLeakCompensation that the user has disabled it). Hence, G F enableLeakCompensation expresses 'at all times, it's always possible to enable leak compensation at some future moment,' and G F !enableLeakCompensation expresses the symmetric property for disabling. The conjunction of both properties (G F enableLeakCompensation & G F !enableLeakCompensation) captures the user's ability to enable or disable at any time. Previous properties that use only e.g. F enableLeakCompensation would fail because that only requires enabling be possible once, rather than at any time.

The input requirement states that the user must be able to enable or disable the leak compensation feature at any time. In terms of LTL with the given atomic propositions, we interpret this as: at all times, it should be possible for the user to select either to enable or disable leak compensation (e.g., by toggling the feature), and that there is no time at which this option is blocked. However, since there is only 'enableLeakCompensation' (enabling action) and 'leakCompensationActive' (feature state), and no direct atomic proposition for disabling, the closest LTL property is to specify that at every point, it is possible for the user to enable the leak compensation (which implicitly means they could also disable, assuming toggling is always available). The property 'G F enableLeakCompensation' asserts that at any point in any path, the action of enabling leak compensation is always possible in the future (i.e., it can always be reached no matter the state). This matches the 'at any time' portion of the requirement. Prior LTL properties like just 'G enableLeakCompensation' would not be suitable, as they would assert that enable is always asserted, not just always possible, and 'F enableLeakCompensation' only claims it happens at least once. This property best captures the English requirement given the available atomic propositions.

The natural language states that the user can enable or disable the leak compensation feature at any time. This means that it should always be possible for the user to take an action to enable, or take an action to disable, without restriction to any particular state or point in time. Translating this into LTL, we express that at any point, it is always possible (i.e., eventually at any state, you can reach a state where...) to enable leak compensation ('enableLeakCompensation') and also possible to disable it (which, by the atomic props present, is not explicitly named, but since 'enableLeakCompensation' likely toggles the feature on, the absence of that means it's off). As per the given atomic proposition, we translate the property as: 'At all times, it is always possible to eventually enable leak compensation, and it is always possible to eventually have it disabled (not enabled)'. This is captured as: G(F(enableLeakCompensation)) & G(F(!enableLeakCompensation)). This property best matches the requirement with the available atomic propositions.
